title: Estimation of Rainfall Infiltration Rates by New Heat Transfer Models and Subsurface Temperature Profiles

research.question: Can time-resolved rainfall infiltration be estimated from subsurface temperature profiles without direct soil-hydraulic measurements?
research.system: A 4 m by 1.5 m by 2.3 m outdoor sand infiltration tank at National Taiwan University, instrumented with eight DTS profiles and flowmeters and forced by three rainfall patterns.
research.inputData: DTS subsurface and surface temperatures, prescribed soil thermal properties, and flowmeter infiltration records for strong uniform, weak uniform, and nonuniform rainfall tests.
research.method: Represent vertical heat conduction and infiltration-driven advection with a transient analytical heat-transfer model, fit its initial and surface-temperature conditions, and estimate seepage velocity by minimizing temperature-profile error with the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm.
research.benchmark: The analytical temperature solution is checked against a Crank-Nicolson numerical solution; inferred infiltration-rate curves are checked against flowmeter measurements in all three rainfall experiments.
research.uncertainty: No statistical interval is reported. The experiments expose structural uncertainty from lateral flow in the sloping tank, temperature noise, soil heterogeneity, and the focus on the saturated portion of each event.
research.conclusion: The estimated and measured infiltration curves have similar temporal behavior across the tested rainfall patterns, while the method requires soil thermal properties rather than soil hydrogeological parameters.

narrative.throughline: Turn heat carried through a wetting soil profile into a time series of infiltration rate, then test that thermal inference against direct flow measurements.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: Infiltration is a groundwater-recharge flux that is difficult to monitor continuously with conventional field instruments.
narrative.stakes: A practical continuous estimate would reduce dependence on costly intrusive sensors and incomplete infiltrometer snapshots.
narrative.resolution: DTS profiles drive an analytical heat model whose optimized seepage velocity reproduces the measured infiltration trend under three controlled rainfall patterns.

background.premise: Rainfall infiltration is an important groundwater-recharge source but is difficult to measure directly and continuously.
background.visibleEvidence: A recharge arrow enters soil while an infiltrometer records only a short isolated segment of the evolving flux.
background.whyNow: Incomplete infiltration histories limit groundwater-recharge accounting.
background.boundary: The paper addresses event-scale infiltration estimation, not basin-scale recharge partitioning.
background.sourceExcerpt: Rainfall infiltration is an important source of groundwater recharge
background.visualObject: soil column with rainfall, recharge arrow, and partial gauge trace
background.motionPurpose: Contrast a continuous subsurface process with incomplete direct observation.
background.stepDetail: Let the wetting front descend while the direct-measurement trace stops early.
background.transitionBridge: Replace the missing flux history with a continuously observed thermal field.

background.premise: Groundwater-soil heat exchange makes the subsurface temperature profile a tracer of water movement.
background.visibleEvidence: A descending water parcel bends a colored temperature-versus-depth profile.
background.whyNow: DTS can observe that profile repeatedly with dense depth sampling.
background.boundary: Temperature is an indirect tracer whose interpretation depends on the heat-transfer model and thermal properties.
background.sourceExcerpt: some studies have used the heat as a groundwater flow tracer to observe the interaction between surface water and groundwater
background.visualObject: temperature-depth profile coupled to a moving water parcel
background.motionPurpose: Make advection visible as a deformation and translation of the thermal profile.
background.stepDetail: Animate conduction as spreading and infiltration-driven advection as downward profile displacement.
background.transitionBridge: Measure the moving profile before solving backward for the flux.

methods.input: Temperatures along eight vertically installed optical-fiber profiles.
methods.output: Time-indexed temperature-versus-depth observations at the experimental tank.
methods.validity: The DTS configuration has 0.1 degC precision and 60 s sampling; profile spacings are 0.1 or 0.2 m depending on cable group.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Abstract; PDF page 4, Section 2.1
methods.sourceExcerpt: this study utilizes a distributed temperature sensing system (DTS) to measure the subsurface temperature profiles
methods.visualObject: eight DTS traces embedded in the infiltration tank
methods.motionPurpose: Populate the soil column with synchronized temperature samples.
methods.stepDetail: Sweep downward along each fiber and leave a colored sample at every sensing depth.
methods.transitionBridge: Feed the measured profiles into the heat-transfer equation.

methods.input: Measured DTS temperature profiles and surface-temperature histories containing observational noise.
methods.output: Preprocessed temperature records for analytical fitting.
methods.validity: The paper requires noise removal before subsequent analysis and cites Rudin et al. (1992).
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 2.5, paragraph following Equation 20
methods.sourceExcerpt: 先將雜訊去除再做後續分析 (Rudin et al., 1992)。
methods.visualObject: Noisy DTS and surface-temperature traces settling into preprocessed records
methods.motionPurpose: Remove measurement noise before the thermal inversion begins.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the measured traces fixed, suppress their noise component, and pass only the preprocessed records forward.
methods.transitionBridge: The preprocessed temperature records become the observations predicted by the analytical model in M03.

methods.input: A trial seepage velocity, soil thermal properties, a fitted initial thermal gradient, and periodic surface temperature.
methods.output: A predicted temperature profile at each measurement time.
methods.validity: The heat-transfer model assumes one-dimensional vertical conduction-advection governed by Fourier heat transfer and energy conservation, with a far-depth zero-gradient boundary.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Abstract; PDF pages 4-5, Sections 2.2-2.3, Equations 1-12
methods.sourceExcerpt: develops a new heat-transfer analytical model
methods.visualObject: analytical temperature curve evolving through the soil column
methods.motionPurpose: Show how a trial velocity transforms boundary heating into a predicted depth profile.
methods.stepDetail: Advance the profile through diffusion-like smoothing and downward advective translation.
methods.transitionBridge: The analytical profile is held beside an independently discretized profile for validation in M04.

methods.input: The analytical heat-transfer solution and the same initial, boundary, thermal, velocity, depth-step, and time-step values in a numerical model.
methods.output: Matched analytical and Crank-Nicolson temperature-depth profiles.
methods.validity: The numerical solution discretizes Equations 1-4 with the Crank-Nicolson method; the reported profiles are nearly coincident for the stated test parameters.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.4, Equations 13-17 and Figure 3
methods.sourceExcerpt: 此數值解應用克蘭克－尼科爾森法 (Crank-Nicolson method) (Crank and Nicolson, 1996) 來離散數學模式
methods.visualObject: Analytical and Crank-Nicolson temperature profiles sharing one depth axis
methods.motionPurpose: Test the analytical derivation against an independently discretized heat equation.
methods.stepDetail: Lock the shared parameters, overlay the analytical and numerical profiles, and retain their reported near-coincidence.
methods.transitionBridge: The validated analytical profile becomes the prediction adjusted against DTS observations in M05.

methods.input: Measured and analytically predicted temperature profiles for a trial seepage velocity v.
methods.validity: The estimation method has high efficiency in calculation; Equation 18 uses a velocity derivative approximated with a forward increment of 0.001v, and noisy temperature records require preprocessing.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Abstract; PDF page 6, Section 2.5, Equations 18-20
methods.sourceExcerpt: combined with the optimization method to estimate in-situ infiltration rates.
methods.visualObject: measured and predicted profiles with a shrinking residual band
methods.motionPurpose: Expose inversion as repeated reduction of temperature mismatch.
methods.stepDetail: Update v, redraw the predicted profile, and contract the residual until the objective stabilizes.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted seepage-velocity trace enters the velocity-to-rate conversion in M06.

methods.input: Time-resolved seepage velocity v and effective porosity n_e.
methods.output: Darcy infiltration flux q_I=n_e v expressed in the rate units used by the rainfall experiments.
methods.validity: Equation 1 carries the advective water-flux factor n_e v, while Section 3 reports the converted infiltration-rate curve for comparison with the flowmeter.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2, Equation 1; PDF page 6, Section 3.1 and Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: 得到時序入滲率之模擬結果如圖 5b 所示，觀測時間為
methods.visualObject: Seepage-velocity trace changing into the reported infiltration-rate curve
methods.motionPurpose: Keep the fitted velocity distinct from the rate quantity compared with the flowmeter.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply the fitted seepage velocity by effective porosity and express q_I in the source's plotted rate units.
methods.transitionBridge: The converted infiltration-rate curve is placed beside the independent flowmeter record in M07.

methods.input: Estimated infiltration-rate time series and flowmeter records from three rainfall patterns.
methods.output: Three event-specific agreement assessments and a visible lateral-flow discrepancy.
methods.validity: The comparison is strongest during saturated infiltration; downstream DTS groups can overestimate vertical infiltration because the tank bottom creates lateral flow.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Abstract; PDF pages 6-7, Sections 3.1-3.3, Figures 5-7
methods.sourceExcerpt: We set up three rainfall events with different rainfall patterns and compare the infiltration rate measured by a flowmeter with that estimated by the present model and method.
methods.visualObject: paired estimated and measured rate curves under three rainfall hyetographs
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal agreement in curve shape and the experiment-specific divergence caused by lateral flow.
methods.stepDetail: Draw each measured curve first, then overlay its inferred counterpart without adding unreported values.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned comparison curves return to the wetting soil column for the source-bounded conclusion.

symbols.meaning: soil temperature at depth z and time t
symbols.transition: The observed curve becomes the target of the analytical prediction.

symbols.meaning: groundwater seepage velocity inferred from thermal profiles
symbols.transition: The trial arrow changes length as profile error is minimized.

symbols.visualObject: residual band and scalar objective meter
symbols.meaning: sum of squared differences between measured and analytical temperature profiles
symbols.transition: The residual band contracts into the optimized infiltration-rate trace.

symbols.meaning: Darcy infiltration flux q_I=n_e v converted from the fitted seepage velocity
symbols.transition: The fitted velocity trace changes into the rate curve that is compared with the flowmeter.

return.visualObject: the original rainfall-fed soil column with DTS profiles, a downward inferred flux arrow, and measured versus estimated rate traces at its base
return.claim: Across the three tested rainfall patterns, the inferred infiltration-rate curves followed the measured curves, and the analytical temperature profiles agreed with the numerical model.
return.claimBoundary: This is a controlled sand-tank demonstration focused mainly on saturated infiltration. It provides no statistical uncertainty interval, and lateral flow, thermal noise, soil heterogeneity, and required soil thermal properties constrain interpretation.
return.locator: PDF page 2, Abstract; PDF pages 5-7, Sections 2.4 and 3.1-3.3, Figures 3 and 5-7
return.sourceExcerpt: The results show that under the different rain patterns, the curves of the estimated infiltration rate are similar to those of the measured infiltration rate.

visual.primaryObject: instrumented vertical soil profile beneath simulated rainfall
visual.accentObject: paired measured and inferred infiltration-rate curves
visual.measuredQuantity: subsurface temperature T(z,t), converted to seepage velocity and infiltration rate
visual.geometryNotes: Use the paper's vertical z axis, eight DTS locations, finite sand tank, sloping bottom, and three rainfall histories; distinguish desired vertical infiltration from experiment-induced lateral flow.
